text: BISCHSHOFEN, a town of the archduchy of Austria, in the circle and 30 m. SSE of Salzburg, on the l. bank of the Salza. Pop. 1,266.
BISCHOFS-LAAK. See LAAK.
BISCHOFSRODA, a town of Saxe-Weimar, in the prov. of Weimar, 7 m. NNE of Eisenach.

BISCHOFSTEIN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of East Prussia, and regency of Königsberg, in the circle and 10 m. WNW of Rossel, near a marshy lake. It contains 2 Catholic churches, and a college; and possesses manufactories of cloth and stockings, breweries, distilleries, and tanneries, and a considerable commerce in yarn and linen. Pop. in 1837, 2,622. B. was founded in 1385.
BISCHOFSWEILER. See BISCHWILLER.

BISCHOFSWERDA, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Meissen, in the bailiwick and 8 m. NE of Stolpen, and 21 m. ENE of Dresden, on a tributary of the Elbe. It contains manufactories of cloth, linen, stockings, thread and lace, tanneries and breweries. It has suffered much from the devastations of war. Pop. 2,430.

BISCHOFSWERDER, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of West Prussia, and circle of Rosenberg, 23 m. SE of Marienwerder, on the Ossa. It contains a parochial Lutheran church, and an hospital; and possesses manufactories of common cloth and shoes, distilleries of brandy and breweries. Pop. in 1837, 1,519.
BISCHOF-TEINITZ. See TEINITZ.

BISCHOF-ZELL, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgovia, cap. of a dist. of the same name, at the confluence of the Sitter and Thur, 16 m. ESE of Frauenfeld, and 12 m. S of Constance. Pop., belonging to the Catholic and reformed churches, 1,252. It is situated on a hill, at an alt. of 1,780 ft. BIS above sea-level; and contains a collegiate church erected in the 9th cent., and the remains of a castle of the same period, built by the bishop of Constance. It possesses manufactories of cotton and dye-works, and an active trade in linen, &c. Its cattle fairs are extensive and well-frequented. The Thur is crossed by a bridge rebuilt in 1487. BISCHWILLER, BISCHWEILER, OF BISCHOFSWEILER, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, arrond. of Strasburg.-The cant. comprises 21 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 25,836.—The town is situated on the r. bank of the Moder, 15 m. N of Strasburg, and 4 m. SE of Haguenau. Pop. 5;927. It contains a Protestant consistorial church, and possesses manufactories of cloth, woollen gloves, oil, soap, and pottery, spinningmills, tanneries, bleacheries, breweries, and maddermills. Its commerce consists chiefly in hops, hemp, and madder, the produce of the locality, leather and tobacco. Fairs are held twice a-year. The fortifications of this town were destroyed by the Imperialists in 1706.

BISENTI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra 1ma, in the dist. and 10 m. NW of Civita di Penne, in a valley watered by a headstream of the Fino. It contains several churches, and a dye-work. It has two annual fairs. Pop. 2,742.

BISENTINA, a small island of Lake Bolsena, in the States-of-the-Church, and delegation of Viterbo. It contains a church, and a Capuchin convent, with a fine park and gardens. This island was the scene of the exile of Theodoric, king of the Goths, and of his assassination in A.D. 534, by order of his cousin, Theodatus, whom he had placed on the throne.

BISENZ, a town of Moravia, the cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 12 m. SW of Hradisch. It has a magnificent castle, built in the 18th century, finely situated on an eminence. The environs produce wine in large quantities, of excellent quality, and maize. Pop. 2,805, of whom 772 are Jews.
BISENZIO, a river of Tuscany, which flows into the Arno, on the r. bank, after a course of 45 m. Several paper-mills, wool-mills, and an iron-work, have been planted on its banks.

BISERSKAIA, a town and fort of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Perm, on the l. bank of the Bisert, 100 m. SE of Perm, and on the great road to Moscow. The inhabitants are required to maintain the bridges, which are extremely numerous in the marshy district surrounding the town of B.

BISERT, or Vysert, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Perm, and district of Krasnoufimsk. It takes its rise in the Ural mountains, and unites with the Oufa after a course of 84 m. from NE to SW. The forts of Biserskaia, Klenovskaia, and Kerkh-Biserskaia, were erected in 1736 on its banks, to protect the district from the depredations of the Bashkirs.
BISER-WALD, a district of Bavaria, in the circle of the Lower Danube, 9 m. NE of Deggendorf.
BISHAKRAH (EL), a town of Nubia, in the prov. of Halfay or Wad Aquib, on the r. bank of the Bahr-el-Azrek or Blue river, 45 m. SE of Khartum.
BISHAM, a parish in Berks, 4 m. NW of Maidenhead. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. 659.
BISHAMPTON, a parish in Worcestershire, 4 m. NE by N of Pershore. Area 2,140 acres. Pop. 410.
BISHARI, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. and 73 m. ENE of Diarbekir, on the l. bank of the Kara-su, 22 m. ENE of Meia-farikin.

BISHARI, or BISHARYE, a tribe of Arabs, who inhabit a district of Nubia, on the E of the Nile, stretching between the 14th and 23d N parallels, where they find pasturage for their camels and cattle in the herbage which springs up in the wadis and exhausted beds of the winter-torrents. In summer, when this supply fails, they descend from the elevated grounds near the Red sea, towards the Nile. They cultivate dhurra and kidney-beans on favourable spots, such as the banks of the Atbara; but they chiefly support themselves on the milk and flesh of their flocks. They are a good-looking, athletic race of people, speaking a dialect of their own, which has no resemblance to the Arabic. They profess Mahommedanism; but are altogether of savage predatory habits, and live in a state of constant hostility with the Ababdehs,
BISHBECH. See BASTA (TEL).

BISHOP AND HIS CLERKS, a small group in the S Pacific ocean, 56 m. S of Macquarie's island, in S lat. 55° 30', E long. 160°.—Also a cluster of rocks off the coast of Pembrokeshire, 4 m. W of St. David's, in N lat. 51° 54', W long. 5° 20'.
BISHOP AUCKLAND. See AUCKLAND (BISHop's).
BISHOP-DALE, a township in the p. of Aysgarth, N. R. of Yorkshire, 12 m. SW by S of Middleham. Acres 5,960. Pop. 107.
BISHOPROGGIN, an island in British N America, near the E bank of James' bay, in N lat. 45° 45', W long. 79°.
BISHOPSBOURNE, a parish in Kent, 4 m. SE by S of Canterbury. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 334. The pious and celebrated Richard Hooker died, rector of this parish, in 1600.
BISHOP'S-CANNINGS. See Cannings (BISHOP's).

BISHOP'S CASTLE, a borough, parish, and market-town, in the co. of Salop, 159 m. NW by W of London, and 20 m. SW by S of Shrewsbury. Area of p. 6,000 acres. Pop. 1,781, of whom 1,510 were in the borough. The local limits of the borough are extensive, embracing a circuit of about 15 m., and being from 3 m. to 4 m. in width in all directions. The town was erected into a borough in the 26° of Elizabeth, and returned two members to parliament. It was disfranchised by the reform act; but is one of the polling places for the members for South Salop. Its fairs are much attended by the Welsh; and the intercourse with Wales is a source of considerable advantage to the town.
BISHOP'S-FONTHILL. See FONTHILL (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-FROME. See FROME (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-HULL. See Hull (Bishop's).

BISHOP-SIDE (High and Low), a chapelry in the parish of Ripon, W. R. of Yorkshire, 11 m. WSW of Ripon, on the N bank of the river Nidd. It contains the market-town of Pateley-bridge. Area 3,480 acres. Pop. 1,937. Lead-mines in this chapelry give considerable employment.
BISHOP'S ISLANDS, a group in the S Pacific ocean, in S lat. 1° 20', E long. 175°, discovered by Kingsmill in 1799.
BISHOP'S-LYDEARD. See LYDEARD (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-MIDDLEHAM. See MIDDLEHAM (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-MONKTON. See MONKTON (BISHOP's).
BISHOP'S - NYMPTON. See NYMPTON (BISHOP's).
BISHOP'S-OFFLOW. See Offlow (Bishop's).

BISHOP'S REEF, a cluster of rocks in the Bahama channel, near the NE extremity of the island of Inagua, in N lat. 21° 15', W long. 73°.—Also a low rocky island in the N Pacific ocean, to the NW of the Ladrone islands, in N lat. 20° 25', E long. 137°.
BISHOP'S-STOKE. See STOKE (BISHOP'S).

BISHOPSTONE, a parish in the co. of Glamorgan, 6 m. SW of Swansea, near the coast. Pop. in 1801, 303; in 1841, 491.—Also a parish in Herefordshire, 7 m. WNW of Hereford. Area 840 acres. Pop. 304.—Also a parish in Sussex, 2 m. NW by N of Seaford. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 288.—Also a 1831, 16,590; in 1841, 27,092. Area of the town- BIS parish in Wilts, 3\frac{1}{2} m. S of Wilton. Area 2,730 acres. Pop. 569.—Also a parish in Wilts, 51 m. E of Swindon. Area 3,330 acres.

BISHOP'S-STORTFORD, a parish and markettown in the co. of Hertford, 10 m. NE by E of Hertford, on the line of the Northern and Eastern railway, by which it is distant 101 m. from Roydon. Area of p. 3,080 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,305; in 1831, 3,958; in 1841, 4,681. The town, situated in a fertile valley on the banks of the Stort, which has been rendered navigable to the Lea, consists principally of four streets built in the form of a cross; and contains many respectable buildings. The trade is confined chiefly to grain and malt. Fairs for horses and cattle are held here. Bishop's-Stortford is a pollingplace in the election of members for the county.
BISHOP'S-SUTTON, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 2 m. SE of New Alresford. Area 3,510 acres. Pop. 517.
BISHOP'S - TACKBROOK. See TACKBROOK (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-TAWTON. See TAWTON (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-TEIGNTON. See TEIGNTON (BISHOP's).
BISHOP'S-THORNTON. See THORNTON (BISHOP's).
BISHOPSTROW, a parish in Wilts, 13 m. SE of Warminster. Area 980 acres. Pop. 296.

BISHOP'S WALTHAM, a parish and markettown in the co. of Southampton, 10 m. ENE of Southampton. Area 5,020 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,773; in 1831, 2,181; in 1841, 2,193. The town, which is neatly built, is situated on the high road from Winchester to Portsmouth, in the midst of a fertile country. The ancient palace of the bishops of Winchester stands on the south-west side of Waltham, and still exhibits traces of its former magnificence. It was originally built by Bishop Henry de Blois, brother to King Stephen; but was altered and repaired by succeeding bishops. The great hall, in the second or inner court, the front wall of which remains almost entire, was 66 ft. in length, 27 in width, and 25 in height, and lighted by five large windows of magnificent proportions.
BISHOP'S-WILTON. See WILTON (BISHOP'S).
BISHOP'S-WOOD. See Brewood.

BISHOP-THORPE, or THORPE-UPON-OUSE, a parish and township in the ainstey of the city of York, 3½ m. S by W of York, on the W bank of the Ouse. Area 760 acres. Pop. 404. Since the destruction of Cawood castle in the parliamentary war, the palace erected here in the reign of John, by Archbishop Walter de Grey, has been the residence of the archbishops of York. The principal additions and alterations were made by Archbishop Drummond in 1763-6; and several new apartments were added by Archbishop Vernon.

BISHOPTON, a parish and village in the co.-palatine of Durham, 6 m. WNW of Stockton-on-Tees. It comprises the townships of Bishopton, East and West Newbiggins, and Little Stainton. Area 5,100 acres. Pop. 473.—Also a township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 23 m. WNW of Ripon. Area 350 acres. Pop. 108.

BISHOP-WEARMOUTH, a parish in the co.-palatine of Durham, forming the SW part of what is commonly called SUNDERLAND: which see. The parish of B., indeed, formerly comprehended Sunderland within its limits; but in 1719 an act of parliament was obtained, by which it was separated and established as an independent rectory. The parish now comprises the townships of B., Wearmouth-Pans, Burdon, Ford, Ryhope, Silksworth, and Tunstall. Area of p. 8,880 acres. Pop. of p. in 1801, 7,806; in ship 3,280 acres. Pop. in 1801, 6,126; in 1831, 14,462; in 1841, 24,206. B. is united with Sunderland by a regular and continued street nearly a mile in length.
BISHOV, or Bichov Santo, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 25 m. S of Mogilev, on the r. bank of the Dnieper.
BISHOV, or Bichov Nov, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 47 m. SSE of Mogilev, near the r. bank of the Soj.

BISHTON, or BISHOPSTONE, a parish in Monmouthshire, 4 m. SE of Caerleon. Area 1,850 acres. Pop. 187.—Also a township in Colwich p., in Staffordshire. Pop. 173.—Also a hamlet in Tidenham p., in Gloucestershire. Pop. 425.

BISIGNA'NO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, in the district and 12 m. N of Cosenza. It is defended by a strong castle, situated on the loftiest of seven hills which surround the town, and contains a cathedral, 19 churches, several convents, a seminary, two hospitals, &c. It is the cap. of one of the dioceses of San-Marco-et-Bisagnano. Silkworms in large quantities are reared in the environs.
BISK, or BISKAIA, a town of Russia in Asia, in the gov. and 265 m. S of Tomsk, on the Bia, one of the head-streams of the Oby, and 10 m. NE of Katunka.
BISKARA. See BESCARA.
BISKUPICE, a town of Poland, in the waivode of Kalisz and circle of Konin. Pop. 380.—Also a town in the gov. and 17 m. SE of Lublin. It consists of about 63 houses. Pop. 600.—See Bischofsburg, Bis-
CHOFSTEIN, and BISCHOFSWERDER.
BISKUPIE, a town of Poland, in the gov. and 5 m. E of Lublin.

BISLEY, a market-town and extensive parish in the co. of Gloucester, 97 m. W of London, and 3½ m. E by N of Stroud, intersected by the Gloucester and Bristol railway, and the Stroudwater canal. The parish includes the chapelry of Chalford. Area 7,980 acres. Pop. 5,339. There are woollen mills here.— Also a p. in Surrey, 4 m. SE of Bagshot, in the vicinity of the Southampton and London railroad, and of the Basingstoke canal. Area 780 acres. Pop. 321.
BISLICH, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, and gov. of Dusseldorf. Pop. 2,000.
BISLIG, a town of the island of Mindanao, in the Asiatic archipelago, situated on the E coast.
BISMARK, or BISMATE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony and regency of Magdeburg, in the circle and 9 m. WNW of Stendal. Pop. in 1837, 1,471.
BISNAGUR. See BIJANAGUR.
BISNEE. See BIJNI.

BISOU, a kingdom of Upper Guinea, on the coast of Gabun, bounded on the NW by the country of the Galbongas, on the N by the kingdom of Aasa, on the E by that of Okaykay, and on the S by the kingdom of Imbiki. It is traversed by the Mounda, which flows into Corisco bay. The cap., of the same name, is situated near the r. bank of the Moundah, 480 m. SE of Benin.
BISPERODE, a village of Brunswick, in the circle of Holzminden, 9 m. N of Bodenwerder. It contains some manufactories of linen, and possesses mineral springs. Pop. 1,020.
BISPHAM, a parish in Lancashire, 2 m. W by N of Poulton, on the coast. Area 3,490 acres. Pop. 2,339, of whom 371 were in the township of Bispham with Northbreck.
BISPO (VILLA Do), a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, 5 m. NNE of Sagres, and 11 m. WSW of Lagos.
BISRAMGHAUT, a mountain pass of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, near Adjygiah, and equi- BIS distant from Kallinger and Punnah. It is about a mile in length, and difficult of ascent.
BISRAMPUR, a town and fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, in the district and 32 m. WNW of Palamow, situated in a mountainous region to the W of the Coyle, and 92 m. SE of Benares.
BISSA-AMADI, a town of W Africa, in Senegambia, 45 m. ENE of Geba.

BISSA'GOS, a group of small islands, off the W coast of Africa, between the Gambia and Sierra Leone, near the mouth of the Rio Grande, between N lat. 11° 40' and 10° 50'. The principal isles composing this archipelago amount to the number of twenty, each of which has a particular name. Those of Bissao, Bulama, Jate, Bussi, and Manterre, are alluvial islands separated from the continent only by branches of rivers. On the other hand, the islands of Gallinas, Arcas, Formosa, Canabac, Carache, Corbele, Genthera, Cavallo, Mel, Casegut, and Cove, are in the open sea. Besides these, there are a great number of smaller islands, of which the most remarkable are Bourbon, Sarciere, Poelon, Papaygo, and Los Parcos. They are mostly covered with wood. On the side towards the ocean this archipelago is covered by a succession of banks of sand and mud, which render the approach extremely dangerous. These banks even extend more or less from the 9th to the 12th parallel, and as far E as the meridian of Ferro. The inhabitants of these islands, called Bissagos, or Bijugas, are a tall, robust race of men; resolute, intrepid, and fond of war,-"a savage race both in appearance and disposition." [Owen.] The Biafaras, a much milder race, who inhabited the islands nearest to the continent, have been driven out by them, and now inhabit the banks of the Rio Grande. An attempt was made in 1792 to plant a colony upon Bulama, one of these islands, which however entirely failed.—Delajoville's Voyage au Senegal.—Beaver's African Memoranda.—Captain Owen's Narrative.

BISSAO, one of the largest islands in the archipelago of the Bissagos. It is situated at the mouth of the Jeba, about 100 m. S of the Gambia river; and is extremely fertile, producing abundantly millet, rice, lemons, limes, and bananas. Its oxen and cows are of remarkable size. The inhabitants, who are called Papels, are enterprising and warlike; the Portuguese also have long been settled here, in great numbers. B. is the great stronghold of the Portuguese slave trade. They have a fort and factory here; and a considerable portion of the wax, hides, rice, and other produce exported from the Gambia is brought from the river Jeba.

BISSENDORF, a town of Hanover, in the gov. and 6 m. ESE of Osnabruck.—Also a village, cap. of the bailiwick of the same name, in the gov. of Luneburg, 10 m. N of Hanover. It has a considerable traffic in horses. Pop. of bailiwick, 5,454.
BISSERKAN, or Basserkan, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik and 53 m. SSW of Bagdad, on the l. bank of the Euphrates, 10 m. N of Hillah.
BISSINGEN, or BESIGHEIM, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, 6 m. NW of Ludwigsburg, and 13 m. NNW of Stuttgardt, on the Euz. It is a great entrepot of timber. Pop. 1,422.

BISSINGEN-AN-DER-TECK, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, in the bailiwick and 4 m. SSE of Kirchheim, at the foot of the Teck. The environs contain marble quarries and works, and a remarkable grotto. Pop. 1,720.

BISSINGEN, or MARKT BISSINGEN, a town of Bavaria, cap. of the seignory of the same name, in the circle of Schwaben-Neuburg, on the Kessel, 30 m. NNW of Augsburg. It contains the seignorial castle of the princes of Ottingen-Wallerstein. Pop. 488. BISSOLIE, BISSOWLEE, or VISAVALI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 102 m. ESE of Delhi, and 30 m. WSW of Bareily, near the l. bank of the Yar Wuada. During the domination of the Rohillas this was a flourishing place, and the favourite residence of the family of Mahomet Ali. In 1774 it was taken and plundered by the nabob Cheja ed Doula. It has regained since it fell into the hands of the British some degree of its former prosperity.—Also a town in the prov. and 100 m. NE of Lahore, and 75 m. NNE of Umritsir, on the r. bank of the Ravee. It occupies an important position on the confines of a mountain range extending to Cashmere, and was formerly fortified. It is now tributary to the raja Runjeet Singh of Lahore. The Ravee is here 360 feet in breadth, and its course extremely rapid.
BISSON (CAPE), a headland of British N America, in Baffins bay, forming the N side of Home bay, in N lat. 69° 15', and W long. 66°.
BISSOU GRAND, or GRAND BASSA, a town and fort of Upper Guinea, in Liberia, at the mouth of St. John's river, 35 m. NW of Trade Town.
BISSOU LITTLE, or LITTLE BASSA, a town of Upper Guinea, in Liberia, 30 m. NW of Grand Bassa. An American factory is established here.—Also a river about 30 m. NW of Little Bassa.
BISSOU HILLS, or Bassa Hills, a range in Upper Guinea, in Liberia, to the NW of St. John's river, about 40 m. from the coast.

BISSUNPOOR, or VISHNAPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, near the S bank of the Dalkisor, and 67 m. WNW of Calcutta. It is of considerable extent, and contains an ancient fort of great strength and magnificence, now used as the residence of the raja. The houses generally are constructed of earth, and thatched with straw. B. is the entrepot of an active trade between the jungle mahals and Ramghur districts. It was formerly the cap. of a large and ancient zemindary, of which only a small portion now remains in the possession of the Raja. The adjacent country consists of a tract of ferruginous soil, rising about 30 ft. above the alluvial plains of the Ganges. To the E it is covered to the distance of 8 m. with a thick palass jungle.
BISTAGNO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 5 m. W of Acqui, near the l. bank of the Bormida.
BISTINEAU, a lake of Louisiana, U. S., in Claiborne co., 35 m. in length, and about 2 in width. Its principal affluents are the Red and Dacheet rivers.

BISTRA, or BISTRAU, a town of Bohemia, cap. of the seignory of the same name, in the circle and 35 m. SE of Chrudim. It contains a castle, and has manufactories of paper and linen. The latter and lint form its chief articles of commerce. Pop. 1,926.
BISTRA, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of the Don Cossacks, which unites with the Donetz, 10 m. ESE of Ekaterininsk.
BISTRIANSK (Sr.), a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of the Don Cossacks, near the r. bank of the Don, 75 m. NE of Nov Tcherkask.

BISTRICA, two rivers of Galicia, which take their rise in Mounts Bisztra and Csorna, in the Carpathian chain, run in a NE direction, unite 2 m. N of Stanislawow, and, after a course of 50 m., fall into the Dniester, 1 m. NW of Mariampol.
BISTRICZA (BANSKA). See NEW-SOHL.

BISTRITZ, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 25 m. SW of Kaurzim, and 25 m. SSE of Prague.- Also a town of Moldavia, on the Goldene Bistritz, 37 m. NW of Baku, and 63 m. ESE of Jassy .- Also a town of Moravia, the cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 35 m. ENE of Iglau. It contains a castle. Pop. 2,000. BISTRITZ, BESZTERCZE, or Nosen, a free, royal town of Transylvania, cap. of the district, and situ- BIT ated in a beautiful valley on the r. bank of the river of the same name, in the Saxon country, 30 m. ENE of Szamos Ujvar, and 83 m. NE of Karlsburg. Pop. 6,620. It is defended by walls, and contains 3 churches, 2 convents, 2 hospitals, 2 gymnasia, a classical seminary, and a printing establishment. It possesses manufactories of linen, cordage, and soap; and has an active trade in cattle, especially Walachian oxen. Fairs are held twice a-year. B. was formerly extremely flourishing, and was the great entrepot of the commerce of the principality of Poland. The district (Besztercze Videke), an administrative division of the Saxon country, is bounded on the N by Hungary, on the E by the prov. of Bukovinia, on the S by the comitat of Doboka, and on the W by that of Szolnok. It comprises a superficial area of 57.2 German sq. m., and in 1837 contained a pop. of 35,842. It is extremely mountainous. The highest summits on its eastern frontier are generally of a granitic character. Calcareous formations prevail in the S, while in the W are the deposits in which the great salt mines occur. The principal rivers are the Szamos and Bistritz. The chief productions of the soil are wood, grain, hemp, and in the valleys, wine. Cattle are reared in great numbers, and the forests, which are of great extent, abound with game. Minerals are extensively wrought in some localities. The climate is very variable, and, except in the valleys, extremely rigorous. The dist. is divided into two circles, and contains I town and 54 villages.

BISTRITZ-AN-DER-ANGEL, or BYSTRICENAD-UHLAWAU, a village of Bohemia, capital of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 15 m. SW of Klattau, on the Angel. It contains the seignorial castle of the princes of Palm-Gundelfingen; and has manufactories of glass. Pop. 524.
BISTRITZ BERG, a mountain on the NE confines of Transylvania, to the E of the district of the same name.

BISTRITZ (GOLDENE), a river which takes its rise on the NE confines of Hungary, in the Gallatz- Berg, runs first E, and then directing its course SE, through the Bukovine and Moldavia, unites with the Seneth 4 m. SE of Baku, at a distance from its source of 135 m. Its principal affluents are the Dorna, Nigra Dorna, Szekuluiaska, and Kis Bistritz, all of which flow into its l. side,—the latter opposite Hunga in Moldavia.

BISTRITZ (NEU), a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 35 m. SE of Tabor. It contains an ancient castle, and possesses manufactories of woollen and cotton fabrics, and linen and paper mills. In the environs are some iron-works. Pop. 2,487.

BISTRITZ-UNTERM-HOSTEIN, or BISTRICA, a town of Moravia, cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 12 m. ESE of Prerau, and 11 m. S of Weisskirchen, on a small river of the same name, at the foot of the Hostein. It contains a fine seignorial castle. Pop. 1,463.
BISTRITZA, a river of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Albania, and pash. of Delvino, which rises in the mountains of Condvouni, and falls into the lake of Varari, 8 m. SW of Delvino.
BISWAH, or Bisooa, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 43 m. N of Lucknow, and 15 m. ESE of Khyrabad.
BISZCZA, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Lublin, 30 m. from Zamosz. Pop. 2,250.

BISZTRA, a town of Hungary, near the base of the Carpathian mountains, 30 m. NNW of Huszth, on a head-stream of the Nagy Ag.—Also a summit of the Carpathian chain, on the NE confines of Hungary, in N lat. 48° 30', and E long. 24° 6'.
BITALTA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Oporto, near the S bank of the Douro, 13 m. ESE of Oporto.

BITCHE, or BITSCH, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Moselle, arrond. of Sarreguemines.—The cant. comprises 14 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 14,207.—The town is situated near the Schwalb, at the base of the Vosges, 18 m. ESE of Sarreguemines. Pop. 3,132. It is of crescent form, and is generally well-built. The citadel stands on a rock commanding the town, and is deemed impregnable. The chief manufactures of B. are china, pottery, and papier mache snuff-boxes. Fairs are held 8 times a-year.—This town was long in the possession of the dukes of Lorrain. It was taken by the French in 1632, but restored by the treaty of Riswich; with the duchy it was finally incorporated with France in 1740. It sustained in 1797 an unsuccessful siege by the Prussians.
BITCHFIELD, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. N by W of Corby. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 160.— Also a township in the p. of Stamfordham, in Northumberland, 1 m. NE of Black-Hedden. Pop. 36.

BITESCH (GROSS), or BITES WELKA, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 32 m. NNE of Znaym. It is surrounded by old walls, and contains the ruins of an ancient castle of great strength. It now belongs to Count Haugwitz. Pop. 1,602.
BITETTO, a town of Naples, in the prov. and 8 m. SW of Bari, in the dist. of Attamura. It is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Bari; and contains a fine cathedral and several convents. Pop. 4,664.

BITIEOU, or PI-TCHOUNG, a province of Japan, near the SE extremity of the island of Nifon; bounded on the N by the prov. of Fooki; on the NE by that of Mimasaka; on the E by the prov. of Bisen; on the S by the strait of Matsu-sima-nada and a peninsular portion of Bisen; and on the W by the prov. of Bingo. Its cap., Matsu-yama, is situated in a mountainous dist. near the centre of the prov.

BITING, a river of Russia in Europe, which takes its rise in the gov. of Tambov, runs to the SW, and unites with several other streams near Ertil, in the gov. of Voronez. Thence it directs its course through the districts of Bobrov and Pavlovsk, and falls into lake Tcherkaskoe, which communicates with the Don, 13 m. above Pavlovsk. Its total length of course is about 105 m. Its banks are covered with magnificent wood.

BITISCHKA-EICHHORN, or BITYSKA-WEWERSKA, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 11 m. NW of Brunn, on the r. bank of the Schwarzawa. Pop. 1,013. In the vicinity is the ancient castle of Eichhorn, a place formerly of great strength.

BITONTO, a town of Naples, in the prov. and dist. and 9 m. W of Bari, situated in a fertile plain. It contains a fine cathedral, several convents, an hospital, and a seminary. It is the cap. of one of the dioceses of the bishopric of Ruvo and Bitonto. The commerce consists chiefly in a species of wine named Zagarello, the produce of the locality. A fair is held once a-year. Pop. 16,235. The victory of the Spaniards over the Imperialists in 1736 took place in the environs of this town.
BITRITTO, a town of Naples, in the prov. and 7 m. SSW of Bari. It contains a collegiate church. Pop. 2,607. The surrounding dist. is celebrated for its wine and almonds.
BITSCH. See BITCHE.
BITSCHHOFFEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Rhine, and cant. of Niederbronn. Pop. 1,083.

BITSCHIN, a town of Prussia, in Silesia, in the gov. of Oppeln, and circle of Tost-Gleiwitz. It contains some iron-works. Pop. 600. BIZ dep. of the Upper Rhine, and cant. of Thann, on the Thuren, 22 m. NNE of Belfort. It contains extensive iron-works and manufactories, cotton spinningmills, manufactories of calicoes, hosiery, &c. Pop. 1,610.
BITTADON, a parish in Devonshire, 6 m. N by W of Barnstaple. Area 5,100 acres. Pop. 78.
BITTBURG, or BIDBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the gov. and 19 m. NNW of Treves. Pop. in 1837, 1,896. It has some woollen manufactories and tanneries.
BITTCSA, a town of Hungary, in the circle of Trencsin, on the Waag. It has an active trade in timber, and is a great entrepot for salt.
BITTELBRUNN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lake, 2½ m. ENE of Engen. Pop. 264.
BITTER, a river of S Africa, descending from the Kamelsberg, traversing the Karroo in a SW direction, and falling into the Atlantic, after a course of about 120 m., in S lat. 30° 40'.
BITTERFELD, a circle and town of Prussian Saxony, in the gov. and 25 m. NE of Merseburg, on the Leber, an affluent of the Mulda. Pop. in 1837, 3,649. It has manufactories of woollens and potteryware.
BITTERLEY, a parish in Salop, 4 m. NE by E of Ludlow. Area 5,610 acres. Pop. 1,098, of whom 204 were in the township of B. Coal and limestone are wrought in the Clee hill in this p.
BITTERN, a tything in the p. of South Stoneham, in Hants, 3 m. NE of Southampton. Pop. 881.
BITTESWELL, a parish in Leicestershire, 1 m. N by W of Lutterworth. Area 2,630 acres. Pop. 495.

BITTI, a parish and town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 15 m. N of Nuoro. Pop. 2,391. The v. is built on one of the most elevated points in the island. It is remarkable for the preponderance of the Latin language in the dialect of its inhabitants, forming a singular Macaronic style.
BITTON, a parish and hamlet in Gloucestershire, 6 m. SE of Bristol. Area of p., 7,520 acres. Pop. 9,338. Pop. of hamlet, 2,413. Coal and iron are extensively wrought in this p.
BIUTAGLABA, a town of Servia, in the district of Semendria, on the E flank of the Peckza mountains.
BIVONA, a town of Sicily, 25 m. NNW of Girgenti. Pop. of district, 2,380. — Also a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 1ma, on the gulf of Santafemia. Pop. 2,000.
BIVADOS, a town of Romelia, in the sanjak of Galipoli, on the coast, E of Silivri.
BIVERI, or BIVIERI, a lake in Sicily, in the district of Catania, near Lentini, which discharges its superfluous waters by the Lentini river into the Ionian sea.
BIX, a parish in Oxfordshire, 4 m. NW by N of Henley-on-Thames. Area 3,130 acres. Pop. 427.
BIXLEY, a parish in Norfolkshire, 3 m. SE by S of Norwich. Area 760 acres. Pop. 110.
BIYSK. See Busk.
BIZALI, a village in the Morea, on the gulf of Nauplia, S of Kranidi.
BIZARD ISLE, an island in Lower Canada, separated from the SW end of Isle Jesus by the Riviere-de-Prairies. It is 4 m. in length, by 2 m. broad. Pop. 757.
BIZE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Ginestas, on the Cesse. Pop. 1,065. There are alum-works here, and a considerable woollen manufactory.
BIZE-NISTOS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Nestier. Pop. 3,191.
BIZERTA, or BENZERTA, a town of Tunis, on the

BITSCHWILLER, a commune of France, in the | SW side of a gulf of the same name, which stretches between Cape Bianco and Cape Zibib, 45 m. NNW of Tunis. It is surrounded with a wall, and fortified towards the sea, but the castle, which is intended to defend the town, is commanded by a hill a quarter of a mile distant. The pop. in 1811, was computed at 8,000, of whom 400 were Jews. B. is annually visited by a number of boats, chiefly Sardinian and Sicilian, employed during the summer-months in the coral-fishery; but its chief trade is in corn and fish. In 1800, upwards of 130,000 quarters of wheat were exported from B. The surrounding country is very beautiful. The land at the head of the gulf is low, but the E side presents a high rocky shore as far as Cape Zibih.—Blaquiere.—Greaves.
BIZIAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Chatillon-les-Dombes. Pop. 1,101.
BIZONNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, cant. of Le-Grand-Lemps. Pop. 1,365.
BIZULI, a town of India, in the Punjab, on the Ravi river, in N lat. 32° 25'. The rajah's palace is a magnificent building, resembling a baronial mansion of feudal times.

BJORKO, or BIRCH ISLAND, an island of Sweden, in the gulf of Bothnia, in the Quarken. It is covered with forests of fir and birch; and has a v. upon it of about 30 houses. A little corn, chiefly rye, is raised upon it; but the chief dependence of the inhabitants is on their cattle and fishing.
BJORNEBORG. See BIORNEBORG.

BJORNKLEVEN, a hamlet of Norway, in Romsdalen, near the Romsdalself, above Ormem. It commands a magnificent view, being elevated several hundred ft. above the river, and having a fine cataract and mountain-stream in front.
BJORNSHOLM. See BIORNSHOLM.
